section: Evaluation of the elastic modulus of steels

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the abstract and literature review of a study on the elastic modulus of steels. Three steel groups are defined: martensitic/ferritic, pure ferritic, and austenitic. Thermodynamic analysis (Thermo-Calc) and regression analysis were applied to ferritic and austenitic groups. Key findings: in ferritic steels, cementite does not affect the modulus, while Cr, Mo, Si, Mn, Cu increase it; in austenitic steels, Ni and Mo decrease, while N, NbC, TiC, Cr increase the modulus, but Cr23C6 has no effect despite statistical significance. The former studies section reviews binary alloy data: Takeuchi found V slightly increases and Be, Al, Si, Ge, Mo decrease the modulus in bcc Fe; Speich et al. showed Co, Re, Cr, Ir increase and Rh, Pt, C, Mn, Ni decrease moduli in fcc Fe, attributing effects to lattice parameter and electronic contributions linked to periodic table position. Speich and Leslie demonstrated that carbon reduces elastic moduli in Fe-Ni-C martensite. Richter's studies indicated temperature-dependent Cr effects in low-alloy steels (diminished above RT), anomalous temperature behavior of high-carbon austenitic steels (lower at RT, higher above 400°C), and increasing Poisson's ratio with Ni content in austenitic steels. Texture effects and dominance of composition over microstructure in annealed heat-resistant steels were noted. Subsequent chunks will detail data collection, thermodynamic calculations, and regression analyses for these steel groups.
key_variables: chemical composition (Cr, Mo, Si, Mn, Cu, Ni, N, NbC, TiC, C, Be, Al, V, Ge, Co, Re, Ir, Rh, Pt, Mn)
mechanisms: lattice parameter change and electronic contribution to elastic constants in fcc iron
texture influence on elastic properties in high-Cr steels
composition dominates over microstructure in annealed heat-resistant low-alloy steels

context_update.summary: This chunk concludes the Former studies section with Richter's observation that composition dominates over microstructure in annealed heat-resistant steels, though his elastic constant data are contradictory. Data collection is detailed: only steels with exact compositions are considered, dynamic measurements are used, typical accuracy is ±0.6 GPa for elastic modulus, Poisson's ratio ±0.01. Temperature corrections to 20°C are done by interpolation. The regression model E=Σ r_i C_i is introduced. Two studies on ferritic steels are presented: Study I (53 batches, microstructure neglected, Table 1, Figure 1) yields coefficients for Cr, Mo, Si (increase), C, Ni (decrease), with R²=0.96; Study II (35 batches, Thermo-Calc phase analysis, Table 2, Figure 2) reveals cementite has no effect, and strong effects from Si, Mo, Mn. Austenitic steel results (24 batches, Table 3, Figure 3) show Ni, Mo decrease modulus, Cr, N, NbC, TiC increase it, and Cr23C6 has no effect. Figures 1–3 and Tables 1–3 provide regression coefficients and significance. Subsequent chunks may build on these coefficients for prediction or comparison.
mechanisms: texture detection via Poisson's ratio (pure iron 0.291)
cementite has no effect on elastic modulus in ferritic steels
Cr23C6 has no effect on elastic modulus in austenitic steels
composition influence dominates over microstructure in annealed condition

context_update.summary: This chunk advances the Results with a Discussion of composition effects on elastic modulus. It first covers ferritic/martensitic steels, confirming Cr, Mo, Si increase modulus, Ni is only effective above ~3% due to phase boundaries, and a higher Cr coefficient in Study I suggests martensite contributions. Table 3 is presented with regression coefficients for austenitic steels (mass% in austenite and carbide phases): notable values include Cr 2.476, Ni 1.887, N 15.101, TiC 7.309, NbC 6.490 GPa/%. The discussion on austenitic steels identifies nitrogen as a strong stiffener that may cluster with Cr; the literature-derived N coefficient of 34 GPa/% is mentioned, but the present study's limited N data (4 batches) causes large scatter. NbC and TiC bulk moduli are cited from literature, while Cr23C6 data are absent. The authors caution against extrapolating from pure-element moduli to solid solutions. A long paragraph on cementite summarizes literature values (177–235 GPa, anisotropic), the role of 7–10% Cr in raising its modulus, and notes that in the present steels cementite contains ~20%Cr and 4%Mn for which no experimental modulus data exist. The cementite regression coefficient matches that of iron, so explicit two-phase modeling was omitted. Mo and Si stiffening effects remain unverified by polycrystalline data. Finally, the usability of the regression coefficients for modulus prediction is affirmed for compositions within the studied phase regions, assuming linearity. Figures 1–3 accompany the analyses. Subsequent chunks may build on these coefficients for validation or broader prediction.
key_variables: mass content of Cr, Mo, Si, Ni, N, C, Mn
mechanisms: interstitial N clustering with Cr increases modulus in austenite
phase boundary in Fe-Ni system governs Ni coefficient validity
cementite elastic anisotropy due to crystal orientation
cementite lattice parameter minimum at 10%Cr suggests modulus maximum
martensitic microstructure indicated by higher Cr coefficient

context_update.summary: This chunk discusses the regression analysis of elastic modulus in austenitic steels, highlighting nitrogen's strong stiffening effect as an interstitial solute, likely due to clustering with Cr. It cites a literature coefficient of 34 GPa/%N at RT, reproduced with large standard deviation because of limited data (4 batches). Reported moduli for NbC (350–500 GPa) and TiC (460 GPa) are noted, while Cr23C6 modulus data are unavailable. The text cautions against extrapolating from pure-element moduli to solid-solution effects. The Conclusions consolidate the regression results for all three steel groups: for martensitic/ferritic steels ignoring microstructure, Cr, Mo, Si increase and C, Ni decrease elastic modulus; for ferritic steels with phase analysis, Cr, Mo, Si, Mn increase E and cementite has no effect; for austenitic steels, γ-iron E=195 GPa (<α-iron), N, Cr, NbC, TiC increase, Ni and Mo decrease E, and Cr23C6 has no effect. These coefficients serve for composition-based modulus prediction within the studied ranges. Subsequent chunks may validate or extend these predictions.
key_variables: chemical composition (Cr, Mo, Si, Mn, C, Ni, N, Nb, Ti)
properties: regression coefficients for alloying elements and precipitates
mechanisms: interstitial solid solution strengthening by nitrogen
possible clustering of N with Cr
no extrapolation from pure elements to solid solutions
